California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 25, L992
Robert D. winston
Mt. Shasta city Attorney
Kirsher, winston and Boston
205 N. Mt. shasta BIvd., suite 400
Mt. Shasta, cA 96067
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-9 2 -067
Dear Mr. winston:
This is in response to your Letter requesting advice on
behalf of Mt. Shasta City Coirncilmember HaroId Sponsler regarding
the councilmember's respons ibi I it ies under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of ine Political Reform Act (the "Act")'1
OUEST ION
May councilnember Sponsler participate in Mt' Shasta City
Council decisions cotceri-ring a pioposed median strip on a street
in iro"t of a busine== orned by- a iource of income, J & A Food
Service, Inc. ?
CONCLUS ION
Councilmember Sponsler may participate in Mt' shasta cj'ty
councit decisions co-ncerning a-proposed nedian strip on a street
in front of a busine=s own"6 by-.f A A Food Service, Inc., so lonq
u= tn" decision wilL not foresieably and materially financially
affect J & A Food service, Inc. 
Even if the decision will' have a
miterial financial effect on J & A Food Service, Inc', the
councilmember may participate if the exception in Section 87103'5
applies.
I Go,r...,rn"nt code sections 8looo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated' 
Cornrnission
regulations appear at 2 California code of Regulatj-ons. Section
ia5oo, et seo^.^ AII references to regulations are to Title 2'
oivision 6 of the California Code of Regulations'
428 J Street, Suite 80O . P.O. Box 8O? o Sacramento cA 958O4-O8O7 . (916) 122'5660


Our File No. A-92-067
Page 2
FACTS
Councilrnember sponsler, in addition to his nernbership on the
Mt. Shasta city countil, operates a nursery and fforist 
business
1n the City of l'ft. Shasta. One of the councilmember's customers
i",r a A F6od service, Inc. ("J & A Food'r) ' 
During the Past l-2
months, J & A Food has been a source of income to the
counciinenber's business in excess of $250'00'
The city council is currently dealing with various aspects of
the city's g-eneral plan. one proposal- currently in the.city's
qeneral plai and paitiatty 
funhed- in the city's budget-is a
ii"o"=.a' roedian strip on i street that runs in front of a Burger
ii.i 
- i.=tirrant , whiih is owned and operated by J & A.Food'. You
stafed that J & A Food did not propose the :nedian strip' although
in. ,"ai."" strip could affect tiaffic 
to and around the Burger
King restaurant-. In addition, you stated that J & A Food had been
l"--ifiiqiiion 
with nearby resid-ents and that a settlenent had been
ieacnea, in part, based on the proposed median'
At the current tine, the city council is considering
elininating the proposed nedian strip from the general plan'
;;;;;;; 
irrJ city'is'concerned that the median strip couJ'd create a
;;G;;;"; 
condition. ProPonents of -the rnedian strip have
"oni."t"a councilmember Sionsler and stated that his financial
a.iii"gt 
with J & A Food is a disqualifying interest with respect
to the decision.
ANALYS I S
Econonic Interests
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official. fron
rnaking, participating in making, or otherwise using hrs or ner
"iii. 
iu f ' p"s i.t i'on to influence a governmental decision in which
tfre otficial 
has a financial inteiest' 
Section 87L03 specifies
iii.t-.-p"nii. "iri"ii-i-iiii 
u financial interest in a decision if
it is r-easonably foreseeable that the decision wiIl have a
materiar financial eifect, distinguishable frorn the effect on the
p"nfi" generally, on the offj.ciaL or a nember of his or her
irnmediate f ani J.y or on :
(c) Any source of incorne, other than gifts and
other than loans by a conmercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terns available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to'
received by or promised to the public officiaf
within 12 monthl prior to the time when the
decision is made.


Our File No. A-92-067
page 3
Regulation
in a pertinent
You stated that Councilmenber sponsler oi,ns a nursery and
florist 
business in the jurisdiction. 
section 82030 provides that
the income of an individual also includes a pro-rata share of any
income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or
spouse owns,-directly, indirectly or benef iciall"y, a lo-percent
iirterest or greater. 
(Tracv Advice Letter, No' I-91-083') J & A
Food has proiided over'izso to the councilmember's business and,
pursuant to Section 82o30, to the councilmember personally'
bonseguently, the counciLmenber may not participate in.aly -
decision which wiII have a foreseeabl.e material financial effect
onJ&AFood.
However, Section 87103.5 provides a limited exception to the
conf I ict-of- interest lar,rs for income received fron retail sales of
good= ot services. Where the retail customers of a business
6onstitute a significant segnent of the public generally, and the
anount of j-ncomE received Uy ttre business from the custoner is not
distinguishable from the amount of income received fron the
busineis' other retail customers, the exception provides that the
j-ncome from the customer may be disregarded for conflict-of-
interest purPoses.
18703.5 which interprets sectj'on 87103.5 provides
part :
(a) For Purposes of Government Code sectj'on
87103:5; the r;tail 
customers of a business entity
constitute a significant seglnent of the Public
general).y if either of the following is true:
(1) The retail customers of the business
entity during the preceding 12 nonths are
suffiaient in nunber to equal 10 percent or
more of the population or households of the
jurisdiction; 
or
(2) The retail customers of the business
entity during the preceding 12 nonths number
at least ten thousand.
For purposes of this subdivision, a customer
of a retaj,l tusiness entity is each separate and
distinct purchaser of goods or services, "'hether an
individuai, household, business or other entity'
If records are not maintained by customer name, a
good faith estirnate shall be rnade to determine what
iercentage of sales transactions rePresent multiple
transactions by repeat custoners. The total nunber
of sales transictions shall then be reduced by the


our File No. A-92-067
Page 4
estinated percentage of repeat customers to yield
the number of customers for purposes of applying
this subdivision.
(b) For purposes of Governnent code Section
87103.5, the amount of incone received by a
business entity from a retait customer is not
distinguishabte fron the amount of incone received
fron its other retail custoners if the anount spent
by the custoner in guestion during the preceding 1"2
nonths is tess than one-tenth of I percent of the
gross sales revenues of the retail business entity
for the preceding fiscal Year'
Thus, the threshold requirement of the exception is that the
retail custoners of the official,s 
business over the preceding 12
nonths constitute 10 percent or more of the population or
househotds in the jurisdiction or nurnber at least 10,000' You
have not provided information pertaininq to the potential
appficati6n of the exception, therefore we can only provide this
gl;leraf outline. 
Ho$/ever, if the exception does apply, the
iouncilnenber vril1 not have a conflict of interest'
If the councilmember has received income from J & A Food
which is not exempted pursuant to Section 87103'5, the
councilmember may not inake, participate in naking, or otherwise
,r=" ti= official 
position to- influence a governnental decision
which will have a reasonably foreseeabLe material financial effect
onJ&AFood.
whether the fj.nancial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governnental decision is rnade
depends on th. facts of each particular case' An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeabte if there is a substantial
Iikelihood that it $iilI occur. certainty is not required'
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, 
it is not
reasona;Iy foreseeable. (fn re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops' 198')
It is sub3tantially Iikely that any alteration to the traffic 
flow
in front of the restauranl wil} have an effect on the profits of
the business.
However, the effect on the councilmember's economic j'nterest
must be material in order to requj-re disqualification. 
The
Conmission has adopted differing guidelines to deterrnine whether
an effect is material, aepending on the specific circunstances of
each decision. For example, where a source of income is directly
before the city council -s an applicant or the subject of the
decision, negu-Iation La702.1(a) provides that the effect of the


Our File No. A-92-067
Page 5
decision on the source of income is deemed rnaterial and
disqua 1i f ication is required. 
(conbs Advice Letter, No.
A-89-177. ) Thus, if J & A Food is the applicant with respect to
the decision in question, the decision is deemed to have a
material financial effect on the councilmenber's economic interest
and Councilmenber Sponsler nay not Participate in the decision.
You ctarified in our telephone conversation of February 4,
1992, that the proPosal for the street rnedian did not originate
with J & A Food, but hlas part of the city's general pIan. Thus,
it does not appear that J & A Food is directly invol-ved in the
decision.
Horrever, the councilnember will stiII 
have a conflict of
interest if the decision wiII have an indirect financial effect on
J & A Food vhich is material. Regulation 18702,2 provides that
the test for rnateriality depends on the financial size of the
business entity. 
Regulation L8702.2 provj.des different thresholds
of materiality for the following:
1. Business entities listed on the Ner'/ York Stock Exchange
or the American stock Exchange, or business entities on the
Fortune Magazine Directory of the 500 largest U. S. industrial
corporatio;s or the 500 Lirgest U. S. nonindustrial corporations.
(Regulation L8702.2 (a) and (d) .)
2. Business entities listed on the National Association of
Securities Dealers National Uarket List or any business entity
with net tangible assets of at least S18,000,000 and pre-tax
income for the last fiscal year of at least S2,500,000.
(Regulation t8702.2 (b) and (e).)
3. Business entities not fitting the requirenents of (a) or
(b) but which are listed on the Pacific Stock Exchange or qualify
for public sale in california and are Iisted on the EligibLe
secuiities List maintained by the california Department of
Corporations. or, business entities with net tangible assets of
at ieast S4,OOo,OOO, and had pre-tax incorne for the last fiscal
year of at least S75o,ooo, with net income from that period of at
least S4OO,Ooo. (Regulation LA702.2(c) and (f). )
4. For any smalLer business entity not covered in one of the
categories set forth above, Regu)'ation L8'702- 2 (g) governs'
specitically, 
Regulatj.on 18702.2(9) provides that the indirect
effect of a decision is material where:
(1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of 910,000 or more; or


(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoidi.ng additional expenses or
reducing or etiminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the anount of 92,500 or morei or
(3) The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or nore '
consequently, if the decisions wiIl affect the gross revenues
of J & a Food uy-ilo,ooo or nore for a fiscal year, or the. value
of J & A Food's assets or liabilities 
by S10,000 or nore, the
councilmenber cannot participate. 
Uoreover, if the decision will
result in J E A Food incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
,"a""i"g or eliminating existlng expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount 6t 12,5oo or moie, the councllmernber cannot participate'
As stated above, it is foreseeable that any changes to the
street that runs past the restaurant will have a foreseeable
financial effect on the restaurant, and it's owner, J & A Food'
I{e cannot deterrnine the magnitude of the financial effect that
will be caused by the decilion on the street rnedian strip, we rnust
leave this factull determination to you and the councilmember
within the pararneters of Regulation tatOz.Z'2
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please flel free to contact ne at (916) 322-59oL'r
Our File No. A-92-067
Page 6
